result:
The first thing that arrives is the smell of rain on hot asphalt two blocks away, riding the breeze through my open window like a courier from childhood. It dumps a sack of memories on the carpet—paper boats in gutter rivers, the hiss of Schwinn tires, my mother yelling that I’d catch pneumonia if I stayed out one more minute. I didn’t catch pneumonia; I caught the habit of standing just outside the awning’s edge, letting the edge of the storm touch me, proving I was real by getting wet. Fifty years later I still need meteorological verification: if the skin stings, the self exists.

Next comes the color cobalt, which has no story, only a torque in the sternum. I’ve never found a word that persuades cobalt to release its grip. Ultramarine sounds like a sea-sick cruise. Sapphire belongs in earrings my grandmother never owned. Simply saying “blue” feels like introducing a galaxy as “a place with stars.” So I keep quiet and let the hue drill its hole. When the hole is deep enough I lower into it every letter I’ve never mailed: the apology to the boy who borrowed my dictionary and never returned it, the fan letter to the jazz pianist who died before I could spell “chromatic,” the breakup note I wrote in my head at nineteen and still edit on insomniac nights—refining the cadence of cruelty, trimming the self-pity, making sure the exit wound is clean even though no one will ever read it.

While I alphabetize regret, the upstairs neighbor begins her evening aria of dropped objects. First a marble, then what sounds like a canoe paddle, then the unmistakable bowling ball. I have never met her, but I know her life in sonic fragments: staccato heels at 7:05, the vacuum cleaner’s monologue every third day, the sudden hush when the phone rings. She is a radio serial whose plot advances only during my pauses. I imagine she listens to me the same way—typing, coughing, laughing alone at puns no one else volunteered to hear. Two strangers orbiting the same drain, each other’s ambient soundtrack, never touching like legitimate planets.

I once tried to record the apartment building’s collective exhale at 3 A.M. I set the microphone in the hallway, retreated, and waited for the sigh of boilers, the refrigerator harmonics, the dream-mumbles through cheap doors. Instead I captured my own blood threading the ears—oceanic, inescapable. The building’s secret frequency was drowned by the pulse I borrowed from my parents, who borrowed it from theirs, an heirloom ticker passed down like a watch that can’t be opened without voiding the warranty. I deleted the file but the waveform stayed, ghosting every future silence.

At 3:07 the refrigerator cleared its throat and I remembered that hunger is a clock older than any number on the dial. I went to the kitchen, stood in the glow of the open door like a pilgrim before a minor shrine, and contemplated the theology of condiments. Mustard: optimism in a squeeze bottle. Soy sauce: liquid dusk. The jar of pickles that predates my last relationship—its expiration date a prophecy I avoid testing. I chose instead a glass of water, the most honest beverage, willing to adopt the shape of any darkness it enters. While I drank, the bulb overhead fluttered a Morse code I refused to translate; some messages are safer left as static.

Back at the desk the cursor blinks like a lighthouse run by an introverted keeper: on, off, maybe I’ll warn you, maybe I won’t. The page is a nautical chart of territories I’ve never smelled but can almost spell—ylang-ylang, Tsushima current, the calcium of whale earbones. I want to sail there on a craft made only of conjunctions, tacking between “and” and “but,” anchoring at the colon where two worlds stare at each other across a tidy abyss. The rules say I must choose one shore, but I’m loyal to the water between, the liquid syntax that refuses conjugation.

Outside, a siren Dopplers past, stitching red noise through the rain’s gray fabric. Somewhere in its wake a heart rate hit 180, an IV bag swayed like a parachute, a stranger learned the weight of borrowed time. I add that unknown life to the ledger I keep in the margins: every ambulance a bookmark in a novel I’m too superstitious to read. The total is meaningless—thousands, maybe—yet I whisper a roster of generic hope: stay, stay, stay. The syllables float up, adhere to the ceiling, dry like opaque shells. By morning they’ll flake and fall, mistaken for ceiling dandruff, swept into the trash with the used coffee filters. But tonight they constellate, a private firmament that believes in me more than I believe in it.

I remember the physicist who calculated that every breath contains approximately one molecule from Caesar’s dying exhale. Likewise, in this room stir atoms that once danced in Virginia Woolf’s headache, in the radiator hiss of my childhood home, in the trumpet exhale of Miles Davis turning his back on the crowd. We are communal air, a republic of ghosts too small to haunt properly. I inhale them, exhale them, add my own carbon confession to the mix: I was here, I was scared, I tried to be kind but sometimes outsourced the job. The molecules accept the disclaimer without comment; they have witnessed worse contracts.

The cat—who arrived years ago with no forwarding address—jumps onto the sill, tail conducting an invisible orchestra. She watches the street as though it’s a slow television show produced just for her. I ask what episode we’re on. She blinks: you wouldn’t understand the canon. True. I lack the whiskers for nuance. Still, I project plot: season finale, the umbrella salesman reconciles with his estranged daughter; spin-off, the skateboard kid grows up to design quieter ambulances. The cat yawns, reveals the pink couch of her mouth, a color Crayola hasn’t named yet because it’s embarrassed by how much it wants to be loved.

At 4:12 the first bird risks a syllable, mistaking the sodium lamp for dawn. One note, off-key, brave as a manifesto nailed to a door no one reads until centuries later. I salute the bird with the remainder of my water. In response the rain upgrades to applause. Somewhere below, the umbrella salesman—if he exists—packs up unsold stock, tasting the metallic tomorrow in the air. I want to run down, buy two umbrellas, open both inside the apartment, defy childhood superstition with adult impunity. But the elevator is asleep, and the stairs creak like old bones negotiating interest rates. I stay where I am, citizen of the in-between, hoarding last licks of night.

The word count approaches retirement. I feel it leaning back, loosening its tie, ready to close the notebook on this shift. I offer it no pension, only the promise that its atoms will be recycled into tomorrow’s grocery list, the apology email, the text that says “thinking of you” at a moment when thinking is indistinguishable from breathing. The cursor blinks one final maybe. I meet it halfway, press the period like a coin on the tongue of a departed ferryman, payment for crossing the small dark water that separates what happened from what we agree to remember.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
